summary: Tests 1-5 are well-constructed: AAA structure, hand-rolled fakes (no MockK), correct UnconfinedTestDispatcher usage, and fixtures that deliberately diverge hfId from storageName/displayName so a wrong-field regression produces a readable diagnostic. However, test 6 does NOT deliver the wiring coverage it claims: it calls `deriveActiveModelName` directly — the same code path already exercised by tests 1-4 — so deleting or replacing the `override val activeModelName = deriveActiveModelName(models, scope)` line in DefaultModelRegistry would not fail any test. The trailing tautology assertion on fixture constants adds zero signal. The J5 hazard test is adequate for the documented concurrency model (sequential mutations + atomic StateFlow map) but the name overstates what it proves. One minor coverage gap: no test exercises a mixed-list snapshot (Ready + Idle entries together), so a refactor of `firstOrNull { Ready }` to `first()` or dropping the filter predicate could pass.

findings.issue: Test is labelled a wiring test for DefaultModelRegistry.activeModelName but never references DefaultModelRegistry. It calls deriveActiveModelName(models, scope) directly, so it is behaviourally identical to tests 1-4 and catches the same regressions — no more, no less. Specifically: (a) if `override val activeModelName: StateFlow<String?> = deriveActiveModelName(models, scope)` is deleted, the property defaults to abstract and the class fails to compile — caught by the compiler, not this test. (b) if someone replaces the override with `MutableStateFlow(null)` or `MutableStateFlow("hardcoded")` or inlines the `map + stateIn` using `.model?.name` by mistake, this test still passes. The final `assertEquals(true, hfId != storageName && hfId != displayName)` tests only local val constants, not production code. The approach note in the PR argues 'regression in either the expression or the `_models` field it reads still surfaces through the helper tests' — that is only true if the override keeps calling the helper. No test enforces that invariant.
findings.recommendation: Add a minimal check that DefaultModelRegistry.activeModelName actually flows from its own `models` StateFlow, without needing Robolectric. Three viable approaches: (1) Reflection-based structural check: use Kotlin reflection to walk `DefaultModelRegistry::class.java.getDeclaredField("activeModelName")`, confirm it is a StateFlow, and — the decisive bit — compare its identity to `deriveActiveModelName(registry.models, scope)` via a lightweight stand-in, OR at minimum assert the override property delegates to the helper by checking field type is not a raw MutableStateFlow. (2) Construct a DefaultModelRegistry with stub LlmModelHelper + TestScope + UnconfinedTestDispatcher — the J5 argument applies here too, so Dispatchers.Default is not actually engaged. Then mutate `_models` via an internal-test accessor or through the public initialize() contract and assert `registry.activeModelName.value` transitions to hfId. (3) If (1) and (2) are rejected, at least rename the test to `deriveActiveModelName_contract` so readers don't expect wiring coverage, and replace the tautological final assertion with a comment explaining it is fixture-validation only. Option (2) with stubs is preferred: it is the only way to catch an inline-and-break-field-reference regression.

replacement.approach: Build a real DefaultModelRegistry with a no-op LlmModelHelper stub and a TestScope+UnconfinedTestDispatcher. Use an internal accessor (mark `_models` internal or add an `internal fun testSetEntries` in DefaultModelRegistry) to mutate state deterministically without invoking the full initialize() path.
assertions: registry.activeModelName.value == null at construction (before any mutation)
after internal-mutation to [ModelEntry(model, Ready)]: registry.activeModelName.value == hfId (the HF id, not storageName, not displayName)
after internal-mutation back to [ModelEntry(model, Idle)]: registry.activeModelName.value == null
(optional) registry.activeModelName !== registry.models — sanity that they are distinct StateFlow instances
replacement.mockChange: Hand-rolled LlmModelHelperStub : LlmModelHelper with empty/no-op bodies (matches project pattern, no MockK). Inject TestScope via constructor or secondary-constructor for tests — already precedented by Task 2's freshDbOpens discussion.

findings.issue: No test exercises a list with multiple ModelEntry instances where only one is Ready and others are Idle / Failed / Initializing. All 6 tests use `listOf(entry(model, ...))` — a single entry. Production code reads `list.firstOrNull { it.initStatus === ModelInitStatus.Ready }?.model?.modelId` on an arbitrary list. A regression that drops the `{ it.initStatus === Ready }` predicate, or replaces `firstOrNull` with `first()` (throws on empty), or broadens the filter to `Ready || Initializing` would not be caught. The tech-spec single-engine invariant justifies `firstOrNull` being sufficient, but the test surface should still pin the filter predicate's behaviour — the invariant is a runtime guarantee, not a code-safety one.
findings.recommendation: Add one test `activeModelName_withMixedList_picksOnlyReadyEntry`: set entries = [entry(modelA, Idle), entry(modelB, Ready), entry(modelC, Initializing)] and assert value == modelB.modelId. Then flip to [entry(modelA, Ready), entry(modelB, Idle)] and assert value == modelA.modelId. Use distinct modelIds per entry so the emitted value pins which entry was selected. This also regression-proofs a refactor where a collection predicate is fumbled.

replacement.approach: Parameterise testModel() to accept distinct modelIds (already supports this via the `modelId` default param). Build a list with three entries in different states and assert the emitted value matches only the Ready entry.

findings.issue: The final assertion `assertEquals("distinct test fixtures must not converge: guard against name/modelId aliasing", true, hfId != storageName && hfId != displayName)` tests only that three `val` constants declared at the top of the file hold distinct string values. It has no relationship to production code and cannot fail unless the fixture constants themselves are edited. It is a tautology dressed as a regression guard. At best it is documentation; at worst it gives false confidence that the fixture divergence is enforced by tests.
findings.recommendation: Delete the assertion. If the intent is to prevent future contributors from collapsing the fixtures, add a `// KEEP DISTINCT: ...` comment at the val declarations instead — or, better, move the divergence check to a `@BeforeClass` / init block that fails fast once rather than per-test-run as a pseudo-assertion.

replacement.approach: Remove the final assertEquals block. Add a one-line comment near the `storageName` declaration documenting the invariant.

findings.issue: Test name contains 'concurrent' but the body drives the full lifecycle sequentially (Idle → Initializing → Ready → Idle → Failed) with synchronous UnconfinedTestDispatcher mutations. No actual concurrency is exercised — no second coroutine, no racing reads. The task's implementation hints correctly note that StateFlow's `map` is atomic per emission so sequential coverage is sufficient for the J5 hazard as documented in code-research. The test is correct for what it checks; the name is just misleading. A future contributor reading this test name might add another 'concurrent' test or skip adding one believing J5 is already exercised against real thread interleaving, which it is not.
findings.recommendation: Rename to `activeModelName_lifecycleTransitions_emitsNullForAllNonReadyStates` or `activeModelName_everyStateMatchesSnapshot_noStaleReady`. Update the inline comment to clarify: 'J5 coverage rationale: Kotlin StateFlow.map is atomic per-emission; every emitted value corresponds to exactly one _models snapshot. We verify this by driving sequential transitions — a real-thread race adds no additional observable behaviour under the atomicity guarantee.'

replacement.approach: Rename test and update inline comment to accurately describe what it proves vs. what it does not (real thread interleaving).

findings.issue: Test 6 is not wrapped in `runTest { ... }` unlike tests 1-5, yet still creates `TestScope(UnconfinedTestDispatcher())`. Without runTest, the TestScope is not tied to the test runner's scheduler, pending coroutines do not auto-advance, and the scope is never cancelled. It happens to work here because UnconfinedTestDispatcher dispatches inline and `stateIn(..., Eagerly, null)` is synchronous enough, but the inconsistency with the rest of the suite is a smell.
findings.recommendation: Wrap test 6 body in `runTest { ... }` and create the scope as `TestScope(UnconfinedTestDispatcher(testScheduler))` — identical to tests 1-5. No behavioural change expected; hygiene fix only.

replacement.approach: Wrap in runTest, use testScheduler-linked dispatcher.

findings.issue: The KDoc on `deriveActiveModelName` mentions 'Task-3 TDD anchor tests' and 'Task 10' references. The function sits in production code (`src/main`) but the doc leaks the TDD-scaffolding rationale into the production surface. Additionally, the function is preceded by a pre-existing KDoc block ('Extracted as an internal top-level function so Task 10's TDD anchor tests can drive the mapping without standing up the full registry graph') which now appears to belong to `deriveActiveModelName` rather than `buildSystemInstruction` — the PR inserted the new function between that KDoc and its original target.
findings.recommendation: Move the new KDoc block so it clearly attaches to `deriveActiveModelName` and the original Task-10 KDoc returns to `buildSystemInstruction`. Rephrase the task-specific references ('Task-3', 'Task 10') in terms of what the helper enables ('exposed as internal to enable direct unit-test coverage of the derivation without constructing the full registry graph'). Task numbers in production code become stale noise post-merge.

replacement.approach: Restructure the KDoc blocks to match the intended targets; drop task-number references in favour of purpose descriptions.

taskRequired.rationale: One major finding (test 6 wiring gap) and several minors. The major is a real regression-coverage hole — a refactor that removes `override val activeModelName = deriveActiveModelName(...)` from DefaultModelRegistry would not be caught — but the compiler and the helper-level tests together still make silent breakage unlikely. Recommend addressing test 6 (major) and the mixed-list coverage (minor) in a follow-up patch to this same task before marking status=complete; the other three minors and the nit can roll into a hygiene pass.
